verses.commentary: Fifth and final Matthean closing formula. The teaching ministry concludes; the passion narrative begins. The five discourses parallel the five books of Moses.

'You know that after two days the Passover is coming, and the Son of Man will be delivered up (paradidotai, παραδίδοται) to be crucified.' Jesus dates his own death by the festival. Passover lamb framing announced.
Chief priests and elders gather at the high priest Caiaphas's palace. Conspiracy localized.
They plotted (synebouleusanto, συνεβουλεύσαντο) to arrest Jesus by stealth and kill him. Open arrest avoided; political caution shapes timing.

'Not during the feast, lest there be a riot among the people.' The crowd's favor (still!) constrains the leaders. Their fear of public reaction shapes the timing — but events will overtake their plan.
Bethany, Simon the leper's house. Healed leper — possibly someone Jesus had cured. The setting is grace-defined.

Woman with alabaster jar of very expensive perfume (mýrou polytimou, μύρου πολυτίμου), pours on his head while he reclines. Anointing gesture — kings (1 Samuel 10:1; 16:13) and the dead (preparation) are anointed. Both meanings simultaneously activated.
Disciples indignant: 'Why this waste (apoleia, ἀπώλεια)?' Same word used elsewhere for destruction. They see economic loss.
'Could have been sold for much and given to the poor.' Apparently noble sentiment masks failure to see what the act actually does.
Jesus knows. 'Why trouble her? She has done a beautiful thing (ergon kalon, ἔργον καλόν) to me.' Reframes the action: not waste, but beauty.
'The poor you always have with you, but you do not always have me.' Echoes Deuteronomy 15:11. Particular moment requires particular response. The poor remain; he does not.
'By pouring this on my body she has prepared me for burial.' The Christological key. She has, perhaps unwittingly, anointed his corpse in advance.
'Wherever this gospel is proclaimed in the whole world, what she has done will also be told in memory of her.' Permanent memorial. Her name unknown, her act immortalized.
Judas Iscariot — 'one of the Twelve' (the specification is the indictment) — goes to the chief priests.
'What will you give me if I deliver him to you?' Negotiation. They weigh out thirty pieces of silver (Zechariah 11:12 — price of a despised shepherd; Exodus 21:32 — price of a slave gored).
From then on Judas sought opportunity (eukairia, εὐκαιρία) to deliver him. The plot becomes operational.
First day of Unleavened Bread — Passover preparation begins. Disciples ask where to prepare the meal.
'Go to such-and-such man — the Teacher says my time (kairos, καιρός) is near; I will keep the Passover at your house.' Pre-arranged or supernaturally known — either way, controlled.
Disciples obey, prepare the Passover. The festival of liberation will become the festival of Israel's deeper liberation.
Evening — Jesus reclines (anekeito, ἀνέκειτο) with the Twelve. Passover seder posture: free people recline (slaves stood).
'Truly I say to you — one of you will betray me.' Mid-meal bombshell.
Disciples greatly grieved (sphodra, σφόδρα) — 'Surely not I, Lord?' Each disciple wonders if it could be himself. The honesty of self-doubt.
'The one who dipped his hand with me in the dish.' All twelve dipped together. The specificity hides while revealing.
'The Son of Man goes as it is written of him — but woe to that man by whom he is betrayed. Better he had not been born.' Sovereignty of plan does not exempt human agency. Tragic verdict.
Judas: 'Surely not I, Rabbi?' (Note: not 'Lord' — 'Rabbi.' He is outside the affectionate address.) 'You have said it.' Indirect confirmation.
Took bread, blessed, broke, gave — fourfold action recalls 14:19. 'Take, eat; this is my body.' Identification, not transubstantiation-debate-launching.
Took cup, gave thanks (eucharistesas, εὐχαριστήσας — origin of 'Eucharist'), gave: 'Drink from it, all of you.' Communal, exclusive of none.

'This is my blood of the covenant (diatheke, διαθήκη), poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins.' Exodus 24:8 (Mosaic covenant blood) and Jeremiah 31:31 (new covenant) and Isaiah 53:12 (poured out for many) all woven. Atonement theology compressed.
'I will not drink of this fruit of the vine again until that day when I drink it new with you in my Father's kingdom.' Eschatological eucharist promised. Communion now anticipates communion then.
Hymn sung (likely Hallel — Psalms 113-118). Out to the Mount of Olives. The Passover meal ends in motion.

'You will all fall away (skandalisthesesthe, σκανδαλισθήσεσθε) tonight on account of me.' Zechariah 13:7 quoted: 'I will strike the shepherd, the sheep will be scattered.' Even the scattering is foretold.
'But after I am raised, I will go before you to Galilee.' Resurrection assumed and announced. Galilee — back to the beginning, but on the other side of death.
Peter: 'Though all fall away, I never will.' Self-distinction from the group. Confidence in his own loyalty exceeds his self-knowledge.
'Truly I say to you — this very night, before the rooster crows, you will deny me three times.' Specificity foreseen: three denials, before cockcrow.
'Even if I must die with you, I will not deny you.' Peter intensifies. All disciples say the same. The corporate vow will fail corporately.
Gethsemane (Hebrew gat shemanim, oil-press) — a garden on the Mount of Olives. The Messiah will be crushed where olives are pressed. 'Sit here while I pray.'
Takes Peter and the sons of Zebedee — the inner circle from the transfiguration. Begins to be sorrowful (lypeisthai, λυπεῖσθαι) and troubled (ademonein, ἀδημονεῖν).
'My soul is very sorrowful, even to death.' Echoes Psalm 42:5, 11. The depth of grief named. 'Watch with me.' Even community in agony is a gift.

Goes farther, falls on his face. 'My Father, if it is possible, let this cup pass from me — yet not as I will, but as you will.' The cup (compare 20:22) of wrath. The Son's request and submission both stated. Filial intimacy, ultimate obedience.
Returns, finds them sleeping. 'Could you not watch with me one hour?' Even one hour. The disciples cannot stay awake while their Lord sweats blood.
'Watch and pray that you may not enter into temptation. The spirit is willing, the flesh is weak.' Diagnostic — they will fail because flesh outvotes spirit when unsupported by prayer.
Second prayer: 'My Father, if this cannot pass unless I drink it, your will be done.' Lord's Prayer language echoed (6:10). His own prayer becomes the model.
Returns again — sleeping again. Eyes heavy. The flesh-weakness manifests as drowsiness.
Third prayer — same words. Trinity-completion in triple intercession. The agony does not abate; the answer does not change; the obedience deepens.
'Sleep and take your rest later. Behold, the hour (hora, ὥρα) has come, the Son of Man is delivered into the hands of sinners.' The hour arrives; their watching is now moot.
'Rise, let us go. See, my betrayer is at hand.' Active movement toward the betrayal, not retreat. The arrest will not catch him by surprise.
Judas arrives with a great crowd, swords and clubs, sent by chief priests and elders. The arresting party is armed — they expect resistance.
Pre-arranged signal: 'The one I kiss is the man — seize him.' Identification by friendship gesture. Betrayal via affection.
'Greetings, Rabbi!' (Chaire, rabbi, χαῖρε, ραββί.) Kisses him (kataphileo, καταφιλέω — fervently). The kiss compounds the offense.
'Friend (hetaire, ἑταῖρε — cool address), do what you came to do.' Or 'Why have you come?' Hetaire repeats the formula used at 20:13 and 22:12 — the address of distance. Jesus is arrested.
One of Jesus' companions (John 18:10 names him Peter) draws sword, cuts off the high priest's servant's ear. Defense by reflex.
'Put your sword back. All who take the sword will die by the sword.' The principle of return-violence. The kingdom does not advance by Peter's blade.
'Do you not think I could appeal to my Father, and he would at once send twelve legions of angels?' Twelve legions = 72,000 angels — overwhelming heavenly army. Restraint is not powerlessness.
'But how then would the Scriptures be fulfilled, that it must be so?' Submission to scripture's trajectory. The cross is willed obedience, not unfortunate accident.
To the crowd: 'Have you come out as against a robber, with swords and clubs to capture me?' Daily I sat in the temple teaching — and you did not seize me. The arrest's cowardice exposed.
'All this has taken place that the Scriptures of the prophets might be fulfilled.' Then all the disciples forsook him and fled. The earlier prediction (26:31) realized in real time.
Taken to Caiaphas the high priest; scribes and elders gathered. The Sanhedrin assembled at night — itself irregular under Jewish law.
Peter followed at a distance to the courtyard, sat with the guards 'to see the end (telos, τέλος).' Curiosity entangled with cowardice.
Chief priests and Sanhedrin sought false testimony (pseudomartyria, ψευδομαρτυρία) — the trial is rigged from the start.
Found none, though many false witnesses came forward. Finally two came (Deuteronomy 19:15 — two-witness rule perversely satisfied).
'I can destroy the temple of God and rebuild it in three days.' Garbled version of John 2:19. Temple charge — symbolic blasphemy.
High priest stands: 'Have you no answer? What is it that these testify against you?' Demands defense.
Jesus silent. Then the high priest under oath: 'I adjure you by the living God — tell us if you are the Christ, the Son of God.' Solemn legal compulsion to answer.

'You have said so. Moreover, I tell you, from now on you will see the Son of Man seated at the right hand of Power and coming on the clouds of heaven.' Psalm 110:1 + Daniel 7:13 combined. Direct Christological claim before the court.
High priest tears robes (Levitical sign of mourning; ironically the law forbade high priest tearing robes — Leviticus 21:10). 'He has blasphemed! Why do we still need witnesses?'
'What is your judgment?' They answered: 'He deserves death.' The verdict pronounced. Sanhedrin had no Roman authority to execute — but the verdict is set.
Spit in his face, struck him with fists. Some slapped him. The Servant of Isaiah 50:6 paralleled — 'I gave my back to those who strike, my cheeks to those who pull out the beard.'
'Prophesy to us, Christ! Who hit you?' Mocking his messianic claim. Blindfolded prophecy parlor game.
Peter sitting outside. A servant girl: 'You were with Jesus the Galilean.' First identification.
Denies before all: 'I do not know what you mean.' First denial. Public, immediate, total.
Goes to the gateway. Another servant girl: 'This man was with Jesus of Nazareth.' Second identification.
Denies with an oath: 'I do not know the man.' Second denial — now sworn. Peter who had said 'do not swear' (5:34) swears against his Lord.
Bystanders: 'Surely you are one of them, your accent (lalia, λαλιά) betrays you.' Galilean dialect. Identity leaks through speech.
Began to curse and swear: 'I do not know the man!' Third denial — most emphatic. Rooster crows (alektor ephonesen, ἀλέκτωρ ἐφώνησεν).

Peter remembers Jesus' word: 'Before the rooster crows, you will deny me three times.' Goes out and weeps bitterly. The grief is real, the repentance begins. Unlike Judas, his weeping turns him back, not away.
